macro_segments.title: Post-Menopausal Woman: The 90-Day Cycle and Pattern Establishment

micro_segments.title: Woman who went 90 days without bleeding and then sees again
Multiple 90-day cycles establish a new pattern of bleeding
Three occurrences of 90-day cycles: from mesuleket damim back to regular woman
Exact vs. inexact patterns: whether precision matters for concern
Gemara analysis: clarifying the order of cases and whether exact patterns require concern

macro_segments.title: Rabbi Eliezer's Position: Any 90-Day Gap Establishes Mesuleket Damim Status

micro_segments.title: Rabbi Eliezer: any woman who goes 90 days without bleeding is mesuleket damim
Baraita account: young woman in Heitalu who stopped bleeding for 90 days
Rabbis' response: that was an emergency situation, not general law
Two explanations of sha'at dachak: famine or large loss of terumah
Rebbe's ruling: relying on Rabbi Eliezer only in times of pressure
Gemara debate: whether Rebbe explicitly rejects Rabbi Eliezer or merely doesn't formally adopt him

macro_segments.title: Pre-Pubescent Girl: Establishing Regular Menstruation Through Multiple Sightings

micro_segments.title: Young girl who has not yet reached puberty but sees blood three times
First and second sightings do not require 24-hour backward concern
By the third sighting, she is treated like all other women with concern
Pattern that repeats: 90-day gaps reset her status to mesuleket damim

macro_segments.title: Pre-Pubescent Girl with 90-Day Interruption: Is It a Complete Reboot?

micro_segments.title: Question: Does 90-day gap make her status like a pre-pubescent girl again?
Rav Giddel's answer: 90-day gap is a complete reboot; first and second sightings need not concern
What if she sees again after 30 days instead of 90 days?
Girl at puberty age: first sighting doesn't require concern, but second does
Acha vs. Rav Giddel: whether the baraita needs two scenarios or one

macro_segments.title: Pattern Establishment: Two or Three Sightings?

micro_segments.title: After second sighting with 90-day gap, third time requires 24-hour concern
Gemara analysis: which position (Rabbi or Rabbi Eliezer) does the baraita follow
Resolution: baraita reflects Rabbi Eliezer on 90-day reboots and Rabbi on two-sighting pattern

macro_segments.title: Stains (Ketamim) Between First and Second Sighting

micro_segments.title: Stain between first and second sighting: considered pure since bleeding is still a fluke
Chizkiyah: stain between second and third sighting is impure since third sighting will establish pattern
Rabbi Yochanan: stain between second and third is pure since pattern not yet applied
Comparison to virgin: virgin's stains are pure due to hymenal bleeding, but young girl has no alternate source

macro_segments.title: Saliva and Sitting Places Between Sightings

micro_segments.title: Young girl's saliva and places she sits: status between second and third sighting
Lenient ruling: treated as pure in public domain due to doubt
Comparison to stain: both considered pure between second and third sighting

macro_segments.title: Continuous vs. Interrupted Bleeding: Counting Multiple Sightings

micro_segments.title: Rav Hilkiya: continuous bleeding for seven days counts as one sighting
Interrupted bleeding (on-off pattern) counts as multiple sightings
Rav Shimi: dripping (continuous but not flowing) is like interrupted bleeding
Clarification: gradual dripping over days counts as one sighting if no breaks

macro_segments.title: Presumption of Purity for Pre-Pubescent Girls; Assumption of Impurity After

micro_segments.title: Pre-pubescent girls: presumed pure; no need to check or worry about bleeding
Post-pubescent girls: presumed impure; must be checked regularly for bleeding
Rabbi Judah: manual checking is prohibited; oil application used instead to avoid damage

topical_tags.term: mesuleket damim (woman who has stopped menstruating)
dayah sha'atah (only concerned from moment of sighting, not 24 hours back)
Rabbi Eliezer (any 90-day gap makes woman mesuleket damim)
Chizkiyah (stain between second and third sighting is impure)
Rabbi Yochanan (stain between second and third sighting is pure)
sha'at dachak (time of pressure/emergency; allows leniency)
terumah (priestly portion; concern for impurity affecting food)
higgia zman lir'os (reached age of puberty)
Rebbe (Rabbi Yehudah HaNasi; ruling on emergency case)
safek tahor (doubt leans toward purity in public domain)
